request.question: Who played bass for the debut album of the band that dissolved in 2011, and had a lead singer what born in 1960

title: Gordon Copley
Regina Zernay Roberts
The Crackerjacks (band)
Michael Stipe
Ivy Jenkins
Mike Davenport
Murmur (album)
No Gods
Joe Raposo (bassist)
Steve Pfauter
sentences: Gordon Copley, born in Pasadena, California, is a bassist who briefly played bass for Black Sabbath.
He played bass on the Black Sabbath song "No Stranger to Love" on their album "Seventh Star".
Bassist Dave Spitz played bass on the rest of the songs on the album.
Copley was also the bassist for Lita Ford's band in early 1985 and joined her on tour after featuring on Seventh Star, leaving Sabbath.
Copley would then later feature on Bill Ward's 1990 album .
Regina Zernay Roberts is an American instrumentalist, singer, and songwriter.
She is lead singer and bass player of Detroit Diesel Power.
She played bass guitar and Moog synthesizer with Scarlet Fever, the all-female backing band that toured with Cee Lo Green during 2010 and 2011.
She toured several years in the New Orleans cowpunk band, Cowboy Mouth; was founding bassist/singer of Los Angeles punk rock band, Méchant; and was bassist for the hard rock band, Halfcocked.
The Crackerjacks were a 1960s Memphis garage rock group.
Band members included organist-bassist Bobby Sowell, lead guitarist David Preola, lead singer Jerry Stamson, and drummer Roy Yeager.
They gained popularity in 1966–67, regularly appearing on Memphis WHBQ TV Talent Party with George Klien (also a popular radio DJ and friend of Elvis Presley).
They won the Mid-South Fair in 1966 for best group.
The Crackerjacks had no bass player; Bobby Sowell played bass and organ at the same time.
The group was short lived due to several reasons.
Sowell was drafted in 1968, Preola also was drafted into the Army, Stampson went into the restaurant business and Yeager went with the group Lobo and the southern rock band Atlanta Rhythm Section.
Sowell was inducted into the Rockabilly Hall of Fame in 2002.
The Crackerjacks are featured in a rock book, "History of Memphis Bands, 1960-72" by Ron Hall, available at bookstores.
John Michael Stipe (born January 4, 1960) is an American singer, songwriter, musician, film producer, music video director, visual artist, and philanthropist.
He is best known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band R.E.M. from their formation in 1980 until their dissolution in 2011.
Ivana Jenkins (née) Vujić (Serbian: Ивана Вујић , born June 15, 1983), known by her stage name Ivy Jenkins is a Serbian-born Canadian bassist.
She played bass for the metal, all-woman band Kittie from October 2007 until March 2012.
She began her career with Kittie by initially filling in for ailing bassist Trish Doan in late 2007, a period which found the band playing concerts in Central and South America.
Before she was in Kittie, Ivy played bass for the melodic hard rock band In The Wake from Toronto, Canada.
In 2008, it was officially announced that Ivy was a full-time member of Kittie and in 2009 she appeared on their fifth studio CD, "In The Black".
Ivy also wrote and recorded bass for Kittie's sixth album, "I've Failed" "You", which was released on August 15, 2011.
Jenkins plays a black Warwick Corvette 5 string bass guitar.
Michael Sean Davenport (born 1968 in Orcutt, California) is an American musician.
Davenport was bass guitarist for The Ataris, joining the band in 1995 prior to the album "Blue Skies, Broken Hearts...Next 12 Exits" in which Davenport co-wrote the song "In Spite of the World".
He also played bass on the album "End Is Forever" and the EP "Look Forward to Failure" on Fat Wreck Chords.
After numerous recordings, tours, and a major label release with The Ataris, "So Long Astoria" in which he co-wrote the song "The Hero Dies in this One" Davenport played bass on "Live at the Metro".
In 2004 Davenport left the band after the Ataris headed in a different musical direction.
In 2005 Davenport appeared in the documentary movie Punks Not Dead www.punksnotdeadthemovie.com "Punk's Not Dead."
Murmur is the debut studio album by the American alternative rock band R.E.M., released on April 12, 1983 by I.R.S. Records.

"Murmur" drew critical acclaim upon its release for its unusual sound, defined by lead singer Michael Stipe's cryptic lyrics, guitarist Peter Buck's jangly guitar style, and bass guitarist Mike Mills' melodic basslines.
No Gods is the debut full-length studio album by British rock band Sharks which was released by Rise Records on 19 March 2012 in the UK and on 20 March 2012 in the US.
The album was recorded in Baltimore, MD, from September to early October 2011 and was produced by Brian McTernan.
The band recorded a total of 14 tracks.
Tony Corrales, formerly of The Exposed, having joined Sharks on a permanent basis, played bass on three songs (James Mattock played bass on all other tracks).
Joe Raposo (born 1970), of Portuguese descent, is the bassist for the seminal California punk rock band Lagwagon and qa engineer lead at Zynga.
He also played bass for a period of time for The Real Mckenzies and Mad Caddies.
Additionally, Raposo plays bass for the San Francisco-based fusion group King City, with fellow Lagwagon member Chris Rest as well as filling in on bass for several live shows with the Dwarves.
Raposo began his career in 1987 at the age of seventeen by joining California hardcore punk band Rich Kids on LSD as their new bassist and remained with RKL until their hiatus in 1996.
He began playing shows with RKL again in 2003 (after they had re-formed a year prior) until their current hiatus after the death of lead singer Jason Sears.
Raposo joined Lagwagon in 2010, replacing original bassist Jesse Buglione.
Raposo also plays bass in the cover band Uke-Hunt.
Steve Pfauter ("Humann") (born 1963, in Francisco, California) is a musician and set dresser and former Bass Guitarist for the Punk rock band The Vandals, from 1980-1984.

Steve was born to German immigrants and attended Los Alamitos High, he joined The Vandals in 1980 and played bass guitar on their debut EP, "Peace thru Vandalism" Steve Human (A.K.A. Human T-Shirt, Humann) designed T-Shirts for T.S.O.L. and also did art work for the Dead Kennedys.
Steve has also played bass in the Original Los Angeles punk rock band Detox, making two full-length albums which featured six songs he wrote while touring the US and Canada.
Later he joined Ken All Night Rocker.
Human also roadied for T.S.O.L. on their first two nationwide tours in 1980-1982.
He was once known for T-shirt designs owning his own silkscreen shop at age sixteen.
He now works in the movie industry as a set dresser and worked on productions such as "Leaving Las Vegas", "High Crimes" and "Spider-Man 3".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Who played bass for the debut album of the band that dissolved in 2011, and had a lead singer what born in 1960
hop1_docs: Islands (Kajagoogoo album) | Islands is the second album by the British pop band Kajagoogoo, released on 21 May 1984 on the EMI label.  This was the band's first album without lead vocalist Limahl; who had been fired by the band in mid-1983 and went on to pursue a solo career.  Bass player Nick Beggs, already the group's main backing singer, took over lead vocal duties, and also wrote the lyrics.  The album was co-produced by the band themselves, now a four-piece group, along with Colin Thurston, who had also produced their debut, "White Feathers".  It is the final album to-date to feature founding drummer Jez Strode.

Rockaholic | Rockaholic is the eighth studio album by American rock band Warrant, released on May 17, 2011.  The album features the band's third lead singer, Robert Mason, who replaced original lead singer Jani Lane in 2008.  Lane returned to the band for a 2008 reunion tour but by the end of the year, he and the band parted ways for the second time.  Lane had previously replaced former lead singer Jaime St. James, who performed lead vocals on the band's last album, "Born Again", and who initially replaced Lane in 2004.

Fuze (band) | Fuze are an English pop rock band who formed in 2010.  The band consists of Ed Alston (lead vocals and guitar), George Kirchner (lead guitar and vocals), Jack Goldsmith (bass guitar and backing vocals) and Keir Adamson (drums and backing vocals).  They were signed by LUMI Records in 2011 prior to the release of their debut album, "That's What She Said".  The band's name originates from the American drink, Fuze.  Their debut single, Hey You, was used in the soundtrack for the UK film, The Shouting Men, which featured Craig Fairbrass, Warren Llambias, John Barnes and Matt Daniel-Baker.  Their second single, When You Come Home, was released in July 2011.  Fuze were crowned Chelmsford's YFest winners in August 2011 after return from their tour of England.  The band are believed to have finished writing for their second album, which is due for release in 2012.

Andalucia (album) | Andalucia is the fourth studio album by Los Angeles rock band Tito & Tarantula, released in 2002.  The album marked several line-up changes in the band, which had previously consisted of lead singer/rhythm guitarist Tito Larriva, lead guitarist Peter Atanasoff, and drummer Johnny "Vatos" Hernandez.  This album featured the debut of lead guitarist Steven Hufsteter – who had previously played with Larriva in the Cruzados, bassist Io Perry, and keyboardist Marcus Praed.

This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like | "This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like" is the debut single of American industrial singer-songwriter Porcelain Black, featuring American rapper Lil Wayne.  This song serves as the lead single from the singer's debut studio album, "Mannequin Factory".  Produced by Moroccan-Swedish producer RedOne, who had originally discovered Black, "This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like" is influenced by the genre of rock, lyrically stating how the listener should be proud of who they are.  The song was released digitally in the United States on March 29, 2011, and managed to chart at number forty-four on the US "Billboard" Hot Dance Club Songs.

Okie Baroque | Okie Baroque is an EP by American indie rock band Blackpool Lights.  After touring to support their 2006 debut album This Town's Disaster, the band dissolved in 2008 for unknown reasons.  In early 2010 after the reunion of lead singer Jim Suptic's other band The Get Up Kids, it was announced on Twitter that Blackpool Lights had reunited and was recording new material.  The album was released online on November 30, 2010.

Rick Savage | Richard "Rick" Savage (born 2 December 1960) is the bass guitarist and one of the founding members of the English rock band, Def Leppard.  He is called "Sav" to avoid confusion between him and the other Rick in the band, drummer Rick Allen.  Savage and lead singer Joe Elliott are the only remaining original members of the band.  The two of them, plus Allen, are also the only members of the band who have performed on every album.
hop1_titles: This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like
response.summary_1: Evidence: "Richard 'Rick' Savage (born December 2, 1960) is the bass guitarist and one of the founding members of the English rock band, Def Leppard." Also, an unrelated band example: "the band dissolved in 2008." Missing link: no passage identifies a band that "dissolved in 2011" whose lead singer was "born in 1960." Next-hop direction: search for the band that "dissolved in 2011" with a lead singer born in 1960, then retrieve that band's debut album personnel/credits to identify who played bass on its debut.
response.hop2_query: band dissolved in 2011 lead singer born 1960 debut album bass
hop2_docs: Speak (band) | Speak (stylized SPEAK) is a synthpop band from Austin, Texas formed in 2008.  The band consists of Troupe Gammage (keyboard and lead vocals), Nick Hurt (guitar and vocals), Joey Delahoussaye (bass and vocals), and Jake Stewart (drums).  The band released their debut single "Carrie" in 2011, and their debut album I Believe In Everything in late 2011.

Mercy Arms | Mercy Arms were an Australian indie rock band formed in early 2005.  The four-piece were made up of Thom Moore (lead vocals, guitar), Kirin J. Callinan (guitar), Ash Moss (bass guitar) and Julian Sudek (drums, percussion).  Their debut self-titled album was issued in August 2008 and peaked at No. 10 on the ARIA Hitseekers Albums component chart.  The band dissolved in February 2009 after four years together.

Rick Savage | Richard "Rick" Savage (born 2 December 1960) is the bass guitarist and one of the founding members of the English rock band, Def Leppard.  He is called "Sav" to avoid confusion between him and the other Rick in the band, drummer Rick Allen.  Savage and lead singer Joe Elliott are the only remaining original members of the band.  The two of them, plus Allen, are also the only members of the band who have performed on every album.

Okie Baroque | Okie Baroque is an EP by American indie rock band Blackpool Lights.  After touring to support their 2006 debut album This Town's Disaster, the band dissolved in 2008 for unknown reasons.  In early 2010 after the reunion of lead singer Jim Suptic's other band The Get Up Kids, it was announced on Twitter that Blackpool Lights had reunited and was recording new material.  The album was released online on November 30, 2010.

Shuffler | Shuffler are an English funk band formed in 2011 with a debut concert at the Jazz Café.  The collective released their self-titled debut studio album, "Shuffler" in 2013.  The band is made up of Daniel Pearce on lead vocals and purcussions, Paul Turner on bass, Rob Harris on guitar and Derrick McKenzie on drums.  It has following in both United Kingdom and Europe.

Back Down to One | Back Down to One is the debut solo album by Jani Lane, former lead vocalist of the American hard rock band Warrant.  Lane was still the lead singer of Warrant when this album was first released in 2003 featuring a band Lane assembled composed of guitarist Matt Cleary, ex-Defcon bass player Mark Mathews and drummer Adam Mercer.  The bulk of the album material would be co-composed by his erstwhile Warrant colleague Keri Kelli.
One Spark (album) | One Spark is the 2011 debut solo album by Gabe Dixon.  He was earlier the lead singer and partial eponym of The Gabe Dixon Band.
response.summary_2: Context confirms Richard "Rick" Savage (born 2 Dec 1960) is the bass guitarist and a founding member of Def Leppard. None of the retrieved passages identify a band that "dissolved in 2011" whose lead singer was born in 1960, so the bassist on that band's debut album cannot be determined from these passages.
response.answer: Cannot be determined from the provided summaries.
